abstract: While neural forecasting has driven significant advancements in large-scale predictive systems, accurate long-horizon forecasting remains a formidable challenge. Most current models struggle with high prediction volatility and prohibitive computational costs as the forecast window extends. To address these limitations, we introduce N-HITS, a neural architecture that utilizes hierarchical interpolation and multi-rate data sampling. By decomposing the input signal into various frequencies and scales, the model assembles its forecast sequentially, prioritizing different temporal components at each stage. We provide theoretical evidence that hierarchical interpolation effectively approximates arbitrarily long horizons, provided the signal maintains a degree of smoothness. Extensive experiments on benchmark long-horizon datasets demonstrate that N-HITS significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models. Notably, our method achieves an average accuracy improvement of nearly 20% over recent Transformer-based architectures while reducing computational overhead by a factor of 50. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Nixtla/neuralforecast.
